the implement run claimed all three #101 findings were non-defects and that logger.exception was unreachable on the BaseException path — an unverified assumption that was factually inverted (logger.exception is called in both except-BaseException arms, http.py:324/389)

summary: PR #102 (/implement of issue #101) set out to disprove three deferred review findings on src/sqllens/transport/http.py via test hardening rather than a production change. The bot's analysis (echoed in the implement summary and commit 450b7c8) asserted the startup/shutdown arms used 'except Exception' which 'cannot catch CancelledError/SystemExit', so logger.exception was unreachable on the BaseException path and the new named-type tests should assert NO ERROR log (caplog records >= ERROR == []). That assumption was wrong: by merge time the implementation had a dedicated 'except BaseException' arm (http.py:303-330 / 374-395) that calls logger.exception at ERROR before re-raising — the contract #100 deliberately documented in docs/internal/mcp-server/transport.md. The /devflow:review verdict was REJECT (CHANGES_REQUESTED): verification checklist VC-1 and VC-4 FAILed and a Critical finding was corroborated by 5/5 agents — all four parametrized caplog '== []' assertions would fail the required 'Lint + unit + transport' CI check, and the rationale comment block was factually inverted (comment drift). The behavioral assertions (excinfo.value is sentinel, sent ==[], aenter/aexit call counts) were sound; only the log contract was wrong. A human (The01Geek) resolved it with post-bot commit bf8c61e2 ('test: align named-BaseException tests to merged #100 logging contract'), inverting the four assertions to expect exactly one ERROR record containing 'interrupted by' + the exc type name. signals: post_bot_commits=1, review_reject_outstanding=false (the REJECT was addressed before merge), ttm 2.2h, no CI failures recorded on head SHA.

suggested_interventions.summary: Add a /implement + review rule that cross-PR semantic contracts (here: the #100 logging contract documented in docs/internal/mcp-server/transport.md) must be re-read from current code/docs before asserting absence of a behavior — disproof-by-absence tests must verify the production arm against HEAD, not against the bot stale mental model

Strengthen review/implement guidance to require running the new tests (pytest) and confirming pass before claiming a finding is disproved — the REJECT here was a test that contradicts the implementation and would have failed CI, catchable by a local run

summary: PR #112 delivered the in-scope O-15 (Dependabot config) and S-12 (resolved wheel lockfile) halves of issue #108 correctly — every pin in-bounds, sqlglot<26 intact, force-include verified by a shadow build. The friction was governance, not code. Two structural failures: (1) the GitHub App token lacks workflows permission, so O-9 (release.yml smoke job) and O-10 (docker.yml pinned cosign-installer) could not be pushed at all and were split to follow-up #111 with a ready-to-apply patch (workpad: "App token cannot push .github/workflows/ ... not a code defect, a delivery-permission limitation"); the bot also could not cleanly split the original combined commit because git reset/--amend/branch -f require interactive approval, so it used revert commit 23f930d instead. (2) The bot shipped an out-of-fence lockfile-drift job in .github/workflows/ci.yml (commit 34c4111) that issue #108 verbatim forbade ("touching ... ci.yml ... will conflict with sibling batches"), producing a self-contradiction: dfr-724784 deferred the drift guard to #114 *because* the fence forbids ci.yml, while the same PR touched ci.yml to ship it. This drew three REJECT verdicts (13:36 untrusted-filer deferral, 15:23 + 15:25 file-fence). The bot reverted the ci.yml job to land only the 4 in-fence files, and a final review returned APPROVE-with-notes (18:39, 21/21 PASS). But signals.review_reject_outstanding is true and the shipped docs/internal/production-readiness-v0.1.0.md still carries stale forward-references — one S-12 row claims the verification half "landed in PR #115" and another says it is "deferred to issue #114", describing a CI guard absent from the final diff. Merged 19:39 with post_bot_commits=0 (all fixups were by the bot iterations) and 2 CI failures during the PR.

suggested_interventions.summary: Strengthen the /implement and review skills to treat an explicit issue file-fence as a hard precondition checked before writing any out-of-fence file, and to forbid filing a deferral whose stated reason (fence forbids file X) is contradicted by the same PR touching file X

Add a Phase-4 docs-consistency gate so forward-references to other PR/issue numbers (e.g. landed in PR #115 / deferred to #114) are reconciled against the shipped diff before merge, preventing stale deferral claims when a planned change is reverted

summary: PR #113 (issue #107, transport-surface hardening) merged imperfect. workpad_final_status is empty and review_verdicts is empty in the bundle, but the formal pr_reviews show a CHANGES_REQUESTED REJECT from github-actions[bot]: 'Verdict: REJECT (one corroborated Critical test-gap: the PRs central error-handling guarantee — eager build_agent warmup failure surfacing as lifespan.startup.failed — is untested)'. descriptors: four shipped doc surfaces + prime_agent docstring + release note claimed the cold-start ~80MB download was moved to server boot, but build_agent only wires objects and ChromaAgentMemory init is lazy so the download still landed on the first query
issue #116 explicitly named the lazy ChromaAgentMemory init as the concern to resolve; first iteration left it unresolved (VC-17 FAIL)
first /review verdict was REJECT; the substantive eager-warmup goal was only fixed in a post-bot commit that added _warm_memory to force _get_collection()/_get_embedding_function() at boot

test_prime_agent_concurrent_with_request_builds_once used a synchronous fake with no in-fake _AGENT_LOCK.locked() assertion, so it could not catch a lock-removal regression despite its docstring claiming to pin it (VC-11 FAIL)
summary: First /review verdict was REJECT: the eager warmup shipped only half of issue #116. It correctly made prime_agent share the request-path _AGENT_STATE singleton (build_agent runs once), but build_agent only wires objects — ChromaAgentMemory.__init__ does no I/O, so the ~80 MB embedding-model download / ChromaDB open still landed lazily on the first query_database call exactly as before. The PR nonetheless shipped four doc surfaces plus the prime_agent docstring asserting the opposite ("cold-start cost is paid at server boot", "your first question answers quickly", release-notes "downloads the roughly 80 MB embedding model while the server is starting up") — claims the code did not deliver, and the issue had explicitly named the lazy ChromaAgentMemory init as the concern to resolve (VC-17 FAIL). The review also caught a test (test_prime_agent_concurrent_with_request_builds_once) structurally incapable of catching an _AGENT_LOCK-removal regression because its synchronous fake never suspends and it omitted the in-fake lock-held assertion its sibling uses (VC-11 FAIL). One post-bot commit ("fix: make eager warmup actually move the cold start to boot") resolved the REJECT: it added _warm_memory(agent) issuing a real get_recent_memories() touch that forces _get_collection()/_get_embedding_function() at boot, corrected all four doc surfaces + docstring + a false _AGENT_LOCK comment, hardened the lock-regression test, added a warm-failure-propagation test, and bound except Exception as exc to surface the exception type in the degraded-boot log. The re-review APPROVED, leaving only a non-blocking doc-precision note (the model download is forced only on first-ever boot via the collection-creation path; a warm restart against an existing collection forces the Chroma open but defers the in-process model load).

suggested_interventions.summary: Add a rule that PRs claiming a performance/behavior change (e.g. cold-start moved to boot) must trace the actual call path proving the expensive work executes where claimed before writing it into docs/release-notes; docs must follow verified behavior, not intended behavior.

Add a convention that lazy-initialization warmup/priming must be proven by a test against the real (non-stubbed) backend asserting the expensive init chain is invoked, since an all-stubbed suite cannot detect that the warm step touches the right method.

descriptors: bot assumed DataFrameComponent column_types would be populated, but the production producer DataFrameComponent.from_records hard-codes column_types={}, so the widget would have sorted every numeric column lexicographically (1, 10, 100, 2)
issue #120 acceptance criterion 'widget supports client-side sort (typed via column_types)' was non-functional in production at the bot's last commit; caught by /review REJECT VC-47

human fix c73aa07 added server-side _infer_column_types / _safe_column_types in _format._compute_table_payload to sniff numeric columns from coerced cell values, with a finite-float guard excluding inf/NaN and SQL NULL/empty cells
summary: PR #121 shipped an MCP App interactive table widget for query_database (issue #120). The bot built the widget's typed numeric sort to key off the DataFrameComponent.column_types field, but never verified that the actual production producer populates it — DataFrameComponent.from_records hard-codes column_types={}, so in practice every numeric column would have sorted lexicographically (1, 10, 100, 2), leaving the explicit issue-#120 acceptance criterion 'widget supports client-side sort (typed via column_types)' non-functional. The first /review verdict was a REJECT (finding VC-47, corroborated by two independent review agents) flagging exactly this. A single human post-bot commit (c73aa07, by The01Geek) fixed it by adding server-side type inference (_infer_column_types sniffing each column from its coerced cells, _safe_column_types degrading a non-mapping producer hint to inference-only, a finite-float guard excluding inf/NaN and treating SQL NULL/empty cells as no-value) so the widget receives a real column_types map; explicit producer types still override the inference. Two subsequent /review passes confirmed VC-47 resolved (47/47 then 46/46 checklist PASS) and the PR merged with no outstanding REJECT. signals: post_bot_commits=1, ci_failures_during_pr=1, no workpad present (workpad_final_status empty), ttm 11.2h. The friction was a single unverified assumption about a vendored producer's behavior that defeated a named acceptance criterion until a human caught it.

suggested_interventions.summary: Add a CLAUDE.md rule for the lifted agent code: when relying on a field/attribute of a vendored DataFrameComponent (or any pruned-upstream type), verify the production producer actually populates it before building behavior on top of it — diff against the producer source, do not assume the field is non-empty

When an acceptance criterion is data-shape dependent (e.g. typed sort keyed off column_types), add a test that exercises the real production producer path (DataFrameComponent.from_records) rather than a hand-built component with the field pre-set, so the gap surfaces in unit tests instead of /review

descriptors: /review REJECT (2 Critical silent-failure findings) left outstanding at merge; human pushed 2 post-bot commits to resolve
export-memory printed green success on an empty/lossy/corrupt store with no warning — a data-loss trap given the documented export-before---clear procedure
iter_all blanket except (TypeError,ValueError,ValidationError):continue could silently skip every row on version skew, exporting a destroyed store as an empty success and re-saving duplicates
MCP import_memory returned a 100%-failed import (saved==0,errors>0) as isError:false success — violates CLAUDE.md isError structured-signal contract
concurrent import_memory calls shared one closure-bound MemoryStore and double-wrote (race), contradicting the documented re-import-is-safe guarantee
summary: /review issued APPROVE (13:12) then a REJECT (15:37) — review_reject_outstanding=true at merge; the REJECT was driven by two Critical pr-review-toolkit:silent-failure-hunter findings (single-source, code-reviewer disagreed). The bot ran the review-feedback loop to "iter 3" but never resolved the two Criticals; the human author (The01Geek) shipped post_bot_commits=2 to fix them after the bot stopped: "fix: address /devflow:review REJECT for issue #122 (silent-failure findings)" and "fix: address /devflow:review REJECT — systemic-failure fail-fast + all-failed isError signal". human_postbot_diff confirms substantive code: (1) export-memory printed green success on an empty/lossy/corrupt store — fixed by adding MemoryCorruptionError + a new ExportResult(text,warnings) that surfaces empty-store/partial-skip/CSV-drops-schema-docs losses, plus a _WHOLESALE_MIN_ROWS/_WHOLESALE_SKIP_RATIO guard in iter_all so a destroyed store no longer exports as a "successful" empty backup and re-saves duplicates on re-import; (2) the MCP import_memory tool returned a 100%-failed import (saved==0,errors>0) as isError:false success and double-wrote under concurrency — fixed with a distinct sanitized MemoryCorruptionError message and an asyncio.Lock single-writer. All issue-122 acceptance criteria verified PASS; the gap was robustness/silent-failure handling, not feature scope. (Note: 2 author-filed deferrals dfr-c01b80/dfr-5548c9 were rejected as untrusted-filer, but neither targeted a Critical so they did not drive the verdict.) No workpad was present in the bundle.

suggested_interventions.summary: Strengthen /review or /implement Phase 4 so an outstanding REJECT with unaddressed Critical review-agent findings blocks the run from stopping — the bot looped review feedback to iter 3 but left the two Criticals for a human; the gate should force the bot to either fix Criticals or explicitly hand off, not silently stop on REJECT

Add a CLAUDE.md rule (or strengthen the existing isError rule) that any tool/CLI path enumerating or wiping a data store must fail loud on wholesale/empty/lossy results rather than reporting green success, and that an all-items-failed result must surface as isError — exactly the silent-failure-on-data-loss pattern the human had to retrofit

descriptors: module-scope `from pydantic_settings import SettingsError` incompatible with the project's own `pydantic-settings>=2.2,<3` pin (export only public since 2.4.0) — broke every CLI entry point on an in-constraint install; caught by the formal review REJECT, not the bot; fixed by the human in post-bot commit 345a687

cli.py:47-50 _SAFE_CONFIG_ERROR_TYPES comment cites enforcement test `test_real_tomldecodeerror_with_secret_scrubbed` that was never added — 4/6 review agents flagged it across two reviews, fix never applied, merged with the dangling reference
summary: Workpad Status was "Complete" and /implement self-reported APPROVE after 3 review iterations, but the formal request-review-on-ready review REJECTed (CHANGES_REQUESTED, 2026-05-19T12:48:59Z) on VC-1: cli.py added a module-scope `from pydantic_settings import SettingsError` while pyproject pinned `pydantic-settings>=2.2,<3`, but SettingsError only became a public top-level export in 2.4.0 — an in-constraint 2.2.x/2.3.x install would ImportError on every sqllens entry point. The bot did not catch this; the human (The01Geek) fixed it in the single post-bot commit 345a687, bumping the floor to `>=2.4,<3` (the lone post_bot_commit). That same human commit also added the missing ConfigBomError pickle round-trip test the REJECT flagged and reworded three comment_drift items. A residual slipped through to merge anyway: the `_SAFE_CONFIG_ERROR_TYPES` comment in cli.py:47-50 cites an enforcement test `test_real_tomldecodeerror_with_secret_scrubbed` that does not exist (the SettingsError sibling test does); 4/6 review agents corroborated this comment_drift across two APPROVE reviews, the recommended fix (add the named test or correct the reference) was never applied, and the PR merged with the broken pointer. The underlying security hardening (#125 deferred finding) was correctly and completely resolved.

suggested_interventions.summary: Add a CLAUDE.md / review-checklist rule: when adding a module-scope import of a third-party symbol, verify the symbol's availability against the floor of the existing version pin (not just latest) before merge — module-scope imports break every entry point on an in-constraint install.

Strengthen the review/implement loop so a corroborated comment_drift finding citing a named-but-nonexistent test is treated as blocking (or auto-verified): grep the cited test name and fail if absent, since the named test is the load-bearing safety net a future maintainer will look for.

empty-string dynamic value not blocked by _is_suspicious_scalar — could expose rows with blank protected columns
reserved metadata keys stripped silently at runtime instead of rejected at config load (doc implied load-time rejection)
_request_metadata caught only (ValueError, AttributeError), narrower than the docstring fail-secure-to-{} promise

summary: RLS PR shipped after a /review REJECT and 5 substantive post-bot human commits. The first verdict (2026-05-19T23:20Z) was REJECT — "1 verification FAIL + 2 Critical doc-drift findings + Important security gap (empty-string dynamic value)". Concretely: (1) docs/external/row-level-security.md claimed SQL Lens blocks "a number when a string is expected" but _is_suspicious_scalar accepts bool/int/float/str interchangeably with no per-rule type check — documented protection that does not exist; (2) the safety/__init__.py module docstring still said "Three orthogonal protections" after a fourth guard (RlsGuardRunner) was added in the same file; (3) _is_suspicious_scalar accepted an empty-string dynamic value, producing WHERE tenant_id = '' that could expose rows with blank/uninitialized protected columns; plus VC-20, _request_metadata caught only (ValueError, AttributeError), narrower than its fail-secure-to-{} docstring. summary: Trivial 9-line docstring-only PR (reword preflight.py module preamble to acknowledge probe_memory's filesystem side effects). Two friction points show up. First, the bot stacked the branch on PR #44's branch because preflight.py didn't yet exist on main — but PR #44 itself had ALREADY reworded the very same module docstring (commit f3f7c0a0, "reword the module docstring to acknowledge that probe_memory does touch the filesystem"). So #69 was redundant work against a moving base: when PR #44 merged to main independently, the human had to merge origin/main into the branch resolving conflicts "in favor of main's canonical preflight feature" and reapply the reword (post-bot commit e220dde) — signals.post_bot_commits=1 plus a merge commit, and ci_failures_during_pr=2. summary: Workpad Status was "Complete" and the /devflow:implement summary claimed "Review converged after 2 iterations (shadow agreed) — APPROVE with notes", but the post-ready formal review (github-actions[bot], state CHANGES_REQUESTED, submitted 2026-05-18T11:10:39Z) returned "## Verdict: REJECT (user-facing doc accuracy + missing input validation)" with 4 single-source Critical findings. The PR nonetheless merged ~5.5h later at 16:44:02Z with post_bot_commits=0 and human_postbot_diff=null — none of the REJECT findings were addressed. The dominant Critical cluster (3 of 4, all comment-analyzer, orchestrator-verified) is doc-accuracy: release-notes.md, external/configuration.md, and internal/agent/memory.md all assert that cfg.memory.similarity_threshold "now actually controls the default similarity floor used when SQL Lens searches its memory" / "controls both", but the text-memory read path (src/sqllens/agent/core/enhancer/default.py:76-78) calls search_text_memories(...) without forwarding the threshold, so Chroma's hardcoded 0.7 still governs free-form notes — the docs overstate the fix scope to cover both memory kinds when only tool-use memory was wired. The 4th Critical (silent-failure-hunter) is a validation gap: SearchSavedCorrectToolUsesTool.__init__(default_similarity_threshold) does no bounds check, so an out-of-range value propagates silently into Chroma (MemoryConfig ge/le validation makes build_agent safe today, but the tool surface lacks defense-in-depth). The core code fix correctly closes both issue-#76 gaps and the 0.0-preservation test is exemplary; the failure is the merge over an outstanding REJECT and the doc claims that describe behavior broader than what shipped.

descriptors: MySQL no-drain regression test (test_huge_select_does_not_drain_after_cap) used a 2-way information_schema.columns CROSS JOIN under the default 30s statement_timeout_ms, so CI interrupted the query at 30s (errno 3024) before the cap-vs-drain assertion ran — connector CI failed and the human had to rewrite the source to a constant-derived 10**8-row digits join with statement_timeout_ms=0
bot did not run the connector suite (skipped without a real MySQL) so the new test premise that the join would be fast-to-first-row was never verified before merge
summary: Issue #58 was a 6-item defense-in-depth follow-up to PR #45; the bot landed all six fixes (truncation signal in run_sql empty branch, SQLite progress-handler ordering, negative-timeout ValueError + 24h config ceiling, specific driver exceptions in tests, literal metadata keys) plus four new tests, and the implementation code under test was correct. The defect was in a test the bot wrote: test_huge_select_does_not_drain_after_cap (item 4, the MySQL SSDictCursor no-close regression test). The bot built the source as a 2-way CROSS JOIN of information_schema.columns and called build_sql_runner without overriding statement_timeout_ms, so it inherited the 30_000ms default and ran SET SESSION MAX_EXECUTION_TIME=30000. On the CI MySQL 8.0 container that join is not fast-to-first-row, so the query was interrupted at 30s with errno 3024 (OperationalError) before fetchmany ran — the connector CI check failed (signals.ci_failures_during_pr=8). The bot did not verify the new connector test against a real MySQL (the connector suite is skipped in the default unit run the workpad cites: "pytest 222 passed, 11 skipped (connector tests need real DBs)"), so the cap-vs-drain premise was never exercised before merge. The human owner (The01Geek) pushed the lone post-bot commit 77bc3d7 rewriting the test: a constant-derived digits table cross-joined to the 8th power (10**8 rows, streams first row instantly regardless of catalog size) plus statement_timeout_ms=0 so the cap, not the timeout, is what is exercised. No /review verdict was recorded (review_verdicts empty, review_reject_outstanding=false) and TTM was ~37min.

suggested_interventions.summary: Add a debugging-checklist note that new connector/integration tests must be verified against a real DB before merge (the default unit run skips them), and that build_sql_runner defaults statement_timeout_ms to 30_000 — tests exercising the row cap must pass statement_timeout_ms=0 so a timeout interrupt does not mask the cap signal
Strengthen the implement skill to require running marked connector tests (or explicitly flagging them unverified in the PR) when a PR adds tests under tests/integration

The 558-char human_postbot_diff changed a single fixture in tests/integration/test_http_transport.py from SecretStr("good-token") (10 chars) to "good-token-0123456789" (21 chars). Per that commit message, this was a "Semantic (not textual) cross-PR conflict: #95's TestHealthz (merged to main) constructs an AuthConfig with a 10-char bearer token, which this PR's new S-13 validator rejects (>=16 chars). git auto-merged the two textually; the test needs the longer token to stay compatible." Root cause: the bot scoped the change "strictly file-disjoint" to its own 3 source files plus their tests and did not search the wider repo for every existing AuthConfig/bearer-token construction site that the newly-introduced minimum-length rule would now reject; the offending fixture lived in a test file (test_http_transport.py) it considered out of scope, and #95 was merged concurrently so it was not on the branch base at implementation time. The rule was introduced but not propagated to all call sites — an incomplete edit surfaced by a semantic merge conflict.

suggested_interventions.summary: When a change introduces or tightens a validation rule (new minimum, stricter type, rejected value), require grepping the whole repo for every existing construction/usage site that the new rule would now reject — including test fixtures outside the declared file-disjoint scope — before claiming the change is complete.

Add a CLAUDE.md note: a stricter validator is not complete until all existing fixtures/call sites satisfy it; file-disjoint scoping does not exempt the author from updating other-file fixtures the new rule breaks. Watch for semantic (non-textual) cross-PR merge conflicts.
